title: Bank Statement Analysis for Trucking and Transportation Businesses
meta: Trucking and owner-operator businesses have unique cash flow patterns. Learn how lenders analyze statements for this industry including factoring deposits, fuel expenses, and IFTA credits.
content:
Why Trucking Businesses Are Different

Owner-operators and small trucking companies generate significant gross revenue — a single-truck owner-operator might gross $150,000–$250,000 per year — but net income after fuel, maintenance, insurance, loan payments, and factoring fees is often much lower. This gap between gross deposits and qualifying income requires industry-specific analysis.

Freight Factoring Deposits

Many trucking companies use freight factoring — selling invoices to a factoring company at a discount for immediate cash. Bank deposits come from the factoring company rather than directly from shippers. Underwriters must recognize these as business revenue, not loans.

Items to Include and Exclude

Include: factoring deposits, direct shipper payments. Exclude: IFTA quarterly tax refunds, fuel card reimbursements, and insurance claim payments.

Realistic Expense Ratios

Standard bank statement programs use a 50% expense ratio. For trucking, this overstates income. Realistic ratios: fuel 25–35%, truck payment 15–20%, insurance 10–15%, maintenance 8–12% — totaling 65–80% of gross. A CPA letter confirming the actual expense ratio is strongly recommended.

Red Flags Specific to Trucking

  • Revenue declining without explanation — may indicate equipment downtime
  • Truck loan payment not visible in statements
  • Daily ACH debits stacked on top of equipment financing — MCA risk

What Lenders Want to See

Consistent factoring or shipper deposits, truck payment documented, reserve balance covering 1–2 months of fixed costs, and no MCA debt stacking. Tools like StatementScrub identify factoring deposits and trucking-specific patterns automatically.
